summary: On Li Ru's advice, burns the imperial capital of Luoyang to the ground and force-marches the court, the populace, and the imperial seals 800 miles west to Changan in the second month of Chuping 1. His soldiers loot the Han tombs along the way.
Li Ru; engineers the capital relocation by playing on the prophecy 'a Han on the west, a Han on the east' - convincing Dong Zhuo that Heaven's mandate has shifted back to Chang'an.
Lu Bu; commands the rearguard out of burning Luoyang and serves as Dong Zhuo's military shield as the coalition closes in.

Argues for immediate pursuit of Dong Zhuo's westward column and rides out alone with his own forces. Ambushed by Xu Rong at Xingyang, badly defeated, and saved only by Cao Hong giving up his horse - the line 'the world can spare me but not you' enters the novel.
Cao Hong; gives up his own horse to the unhorsed Cao Cao after the Xingyang rout - 'the world can spare me but not you' - saving Cao Cao's life.
Xu Rong; Dong Zhuo's general left to harass the pursuit out of Luoyang. Routs Cao Cao at Xingyang in the chapter's standout ambush.

Enters Luoyang first among the coalition, finds the Han imperial seal in a well near Jianzhang Hall, decides to keep it secret, and breaks camp to take it home. Cuts his way through Cai Mao and Kuai Yue's blockade on the road south.
Receives word of Sun Jian's secret seal and writes to Liu Biao asking him to intercept Sun Jian's column - the rupture that effectively dissolves the coalition.
Liu Biao; called on by Yuan Shao to bar Sun Jian's road home. Sends Kuai Yue and Cai Mao with 10,000 troops; the engagement makes the Liu-Sun feud permanent.
Kuai Yue; Liu Biao's adviser sent to intercept Sun Jian and demand the imperial seal.
Kuai Liang; Kuai Yue's brother; senior adviser among Liu Biao's three famous helpers.
Cai Mao; Liu Biao's brother-in-law; one of the two field commanders sent against Sun Jian. Struck off his horse by Huang Gai's iron whip.
Huang Gai; cuts down Cai Mao with his iron whip to clear Sun Jian's column through Liu Biao's blockade.
Cheng Pu; serves under Sun Jian in the breakthrough south from Luoyang.
Han Dang; serves under Sun Jian in the breakthrough.
Guo Si; one of Dong Zhuo's named generals covering the retreat to Changan.
Li Jue; covers the retreat to Changan.
Xiahou Dun; rides with Cao Cao in the disastrous pursuit and shares in the Xingyang defeat.
Xiahou Yuan; one of the generals fighting alongside Cao Cao in the Xingyang ambush.
Cao Ren; part of Cao Cao's pursuit force at Xingyang.
Li Dian; part of Cao Cao's pursuit force at Xingyang.
Yue Jin; part of Cao Cao's pursuit force at Xingyang.
Liu Dai; one of the coalition lords who declines to march further west, accelerating the coalition's collapse.
Qiao Mao; coalition lord at the council; refuses to follow Cao Cao west.
Gongsun Zan; pulls back from the coalition after Sun Jian's seal-keeping is discovered.
Yuan Shu; named among the coalition lords now disinclined to continue the campaign.
Emperor Xian; forced to abandon Luoyang in his uncle Dong Zhuo's column for the relocation to Changan.
Yan Liang; one of Yuan Shao's named generals as the coalition fractures.
Wen Chou; one of Yuan Shao's named generals as the coalition fractures.
Liu Bei; remains with Gongsun Zan as the coalition fractures, watching the collapse of the eastern alliance.
Zhang Fei; with Liu Bei in Gongsun Zan's camp.
Yang Biao; senior minister forced to follow Dong Zhuo's court west to Changan.
Huang Wan; minister killed when he protests the burning of Luoyang.
Wu Qiong; minister in Dong Zhuo's circle during the relocation.
Zhou Bi; minister in Dong Zhuo's circle during the relocation.
Xun Shuang; minister forced to migrate to Changan with the court.
Chen Xiang; minister whose protest against the relocation is overridden.
Kong Yu; minister caught up in the relocation.
Cen Zhi; one of Liu Biao's three famous helpers (with the Kuai brothers and Cai Mao).
Tan Fu; named among Liu Biao's circle in Jingzhou.
Zhang Jian; named among Liu Biao's Jingzhou circle.
Zhao Cen; named among the Jingzhou circle.
Han founder Liu Bang; cited in Li Ru's 'Han on the west, Han on the east' prophecy paragraph.
Liu Xiu; cited in the same prophecy paragraph as the founder of the Latter Han at Luoyang.
Wang Mang; cited in the same historical-recap paragraph.
Bian He; historical reference to the discoverer of the original imperial jade - invoked when Sun Jian recognises the seal.
Zi Ying; last ruler of Qin; named in the imperial-seal lineage flashback.
Li Si; Qin minister credited with cutting the original eight-character seal inscription - flashback detail.
Wang Xun; historical figure cited in the seal's lineage.
Su Xian; named in the seal's lineage.
Lady Yuan; named in the seal's lineage flashback.
Fan Pang; minor figure named in passing in the chapter's catalogue.
Fan Kang; minor figure named in passing.

Yuan Shao; engineers the takeover of Jizhou by sending word to Gongsun Zan to attack Han Fu jointly, then sweeping in 'to mediate' and absorbing the whole region. When Gongsun Zan's brother arrives to claim a share, Yuan Shao stalls; the dispute escalates to open war at the Pan River.
Han Fu; Imperial Protector of Jizhou; persuaded by Xun Chang to invite Yuan Shao in 'to administer' the region and is stripped of all power once Yuan Shao's confidants take over. Flees to Zhang Miao.
Xun Chang; Han Fu's adviser; pressures Han Fu to hand Jizhou to Yuan Shao.
Xin Ping; Han Fu's adviser; partners with Xun Chang.
Geng Wu; warns Han Fu that admitting Yuan Shao is 'letting a tiger into the sheepfold'; ignored, hides in the suburbs and tries to assassinate Yuan Shao on arrival. Killed by Yan Liang.
Guan Chun; partners with Geng Wu in the failed assassination attempt; killed by Wen Chou.
Yan Liang; beheads Geng Wu in Yuan Shao's first sweep through Jizhou.
Wen Chou; beheads Guan Chun alongside Yan Liang's killing of Geng Wu.

Gongsun Zan; tricked into attacking Jizhou by Yuan Shao's promise of joint partition; receives nothing, sends his brother to demand a share, and after his brother is killed marches on the Pan River. Suffers a major defeat there before Liu Bei's relief column closes the chapter's first half.
Gongsun Yue; Gongsun Zan's brother; sent to demand the promised half of Jizhou. Killed in an ambush that Yuan Shao deniably arranges - the trigger for the Pan River battle.
Qu Yi; Yuan Shao's general who hides 800 crossbowmen behind shield-bearers and slaughters Gongsun Zan's white-horse cavalry - the trick that breaks Gongsun Zan's army at the Pan River.

Zhao Yun's debut chapter - a tall, dignified young warrior from Changshan who has just left Yuan Shao in disgust. Saves Gongsun Zan from death under the Yuan Shao army, takes service with him, and immediately makes friends with Liu Bei after the relief column arrives.

Arrives with his brothers and Gongsun Zan's troops at the Pan River; their charge breaks Yan Liang and Wen Chou's encirclement and saves Gongsun Zan. Meets Zhao Yun for the first time; the two part reluctantly when the emperor's truce envoy ends the campaign.
Charges with his brothers at the Pan River to rescue Gongsun Zan, helping break Yuan Shao's encirclement.
Charges with his brothers at the Pan River; relieves Gongsun Zan.
Ma Midi; envoy sent by Emperor Xian (via Dong Zhuo's nominal authority) to broker the truce between Yuan Shao and Gongsun Zan after the Pan River.
Zhao Qi; co-envoy with Ma Midi for the truce.
Dong Zhuo; signs off on the truce envoys to the eastern princes - happy to see his enemies bleed each other.
Li Ru; pushes Dong Zhuo to issue the truce decree so Yuan Shao and Gongsun Zan continue weakening each other.

Sun Jian; turns south to settle scores with Liu Biao for the Luoyang blockade. Outflanks Huang Zu at the Han River, pursues into the western hills outside Xiangyang - and is killed by an arrow-and-stone barrage from an ambush set by Lü Gong. Dies aged 37.

Sun Ce; eighteen years old; rides with his father against Liu Biao. After Sun Jian's death he negotiates the body's return by trading Huang Zu and brings Sun Jian's coffin home - the start of his arc.
Sun Quan; named among Sun Jian's children at the start of the campaign.
Sun Jing; Sun Jian's younger brother; helps Sun Ce conduct the funeral and shore up the family after the Xiangyang disaster.
Sun Kuang; one of Sun Jian's sons; named in the family roll.
Sun Lang; another of Sun Jian's sons; named in the family roll.
Sun Yi; one of Sun Jian's sons; named in the family roll.
Sun Ren; Sun Jian relative named in passing.
Sun Hu; Sun Jian relative named in passing.
Liu Biao; receives Yuan Shao's letter denouncing Sun Jian and sends Huang Zu to harass Sun Jian's crossing - provoking the campaign that ends with Sun Jian dead in the western hills near Xiangyang.
Kuai Yue; Liu Biao's general engaging Sun Jian's column from the south bank.
Kuai Liang; Liu Biao's senior adviser; suggests setting the ambush in the hills that kills Sun Jian.
Lü Gong; Liu Biao's general; sets the boulder-and-arrow ambush in the western hills that kills Sun Jian.
Huang Zu; Liu Biao's commander captured by Sun Ce and exchanged for Sun Jian's body - the start of a feud that runs into the 30s.
Huan Jie; Sun Ce's envoy to Liu Biao to negotiate the body-for-prisoner exchange.
Cheng Pu; serves with Sun Jian throughout the Liu Biao campaign and helps Sun Ce conduct the retreat.
Han Dang; serves with Sun Jian throughout the Liu Biao campaign.
Huang Gai; serves with Sun Jian and partners in the retreat after the ambush.
Cai Mao; mentioned in passing in the Liu Biao section of the chapter.
Ju Shou; named among Yuan Shao's growing brain trust at Jizhou.
Tian Feng; named among Yuan Shao's new advisers.
Xu You; named among Yuan Shao's new advisers.
Peng Ji; Yuan Shao adviser who first proposes seizing Jizhou outright.
Yuan Shu; named in passing in the chapter's catalogue of regional players.
Zhang Miao; Han Fu's eventual refuge after his power evaporates.
Chen Sheng; minor figure named in passing.
Yan Gang; minor figure named in passing.
Zhang Hu; minor figure named in passing.

Wang Yun; sets the entire chained-scheme plot in motion. Weeps in his garden under the moon at the Han's coming fall, recruits the singing-girl Diao Chan, and at her offer to die for him persuades her to play Lu Bu and Dong Zhuo against each other. Promises her to Lu Bu as a wife, then to Dong Zhuo as a concubine - engineering the moment that will close in chapter 9.

Diao Chan; sixteen-year-old singing-girl in Wang Yun's household. Volunteers to die for him to repay his care for her, then plays Lu Bu and Dong Zhuo with consummate skill - weeping in the right place for each, fueling Lu Bu's jealousy while flattering Dong Zhuo. One of the novel's defining women.

Dong Zhuo; accepts Diao Chan from Wang Yun as a concubine and takes her to his new fortified city of Meiwo. By chapter's end he and Lu Bu have already had their first violent collision over her - Dong Zhuo throwing his halberd at Lu Bu in the Phoenix Pavilion garden.

Lu Bu; first promised Diao Chan as wife by Wang Yun, then enraged when Dong Zhuo takes her. Steals into the Phoenix Pavilion to meet her in secret; Dong Zhuo catches them, hurls his halberd, and Lu Bu flees - falling at the gate into Li Ru. The lovers' set piece.

Li Ru; tries throughout the chapter to defuse the Lu Bu / Dong Zhuo collision, picks Dong Zhuo up off the ground after the Phoenix Pavilion incident, and counsels giving Diao Chan to Lu Bu as the only way out - counsel Dong Zhuo refuses.
Dong Huang; Dong Zhuo's nephew installed as Court Counselor and palace guard commander when Dong Zhuo distributes spoils to his clan in the chapter's opening section.
Dong Min; Dong Zhuo's younger brother; created Lord of Huazhou.
Zhang Wen; Minister of Works; dragged out and beheaded mid-banquet on a flimsy charge - the head presented on a platter to silence the room. The chapter's headline image of Dong Zhuo's terror.
Huan Jie; envoy who delivers Sun Jian's body home to Sun Ce; brief return appearance after chapter 7.
Huang Zu; released by Sun Ce in the body-exchange that closes the Liu Biao chapter.
Liu Biao; receives Sun Ce's exchange terms and returns Sun Jian's coffin.
Kuai Liang; advises Liu Biao to attack the weakened Sun family rather than make peace - Liu Biao ignores him.
Sun Jian; named in passing as the now-buried father whose body Sun Ce has brought home.
Sun Ce; receives Sun Jian's body and buries him on the plain of Que; sets about quietly building strength in Changsha while gathering able people.
Yuan Shu; named in passing as Sun Ce moves through territories he formally owes allegiance to.
Emperor Xian; the powerless boy emperor whom Dong Zhuo now styles 'Imperial Rector' (Shang Fu - 'father to the emperor').
Yi Yin; ancient minister cited as a model when Dong Zhuo gives himself the Shang Fu title.
Shun; ancient sage emperor named in the chapter's opening rhetoric on succession.
Yao; ancient sage emperor named alongside Shun.
Yu the Great; named in the same opening rhetorical roll.
Dan; ancient figure named in the chapter's opening rhetorical roll.

Persuaded by Wang Yun (and Diao Chan's weeping) to switch sides; stops Dong Zhuo's chariot at the Hall of Virtue and pierces him through with the halberd as the emperor's nominal abdication invitation is being read aloud. Takes Dong Zhuo's seal and Diao Chan for himself - the killing that defines his arc.

Lured to Changan with the false promise of imperial abdication; Lu Bu kills him at the Hall of Virtue. His corpse is exposed in the marketplace; passing soldiers stuff lighted wicks into his belly-fat. The chapter pivots on his death.

Wang Yun; coordinates the killing - gives Lu Bu the imperial mandate to strike, raises 'Long live the Han!' over Dong Zhuo's body. By chapter's end the Li Jue and Guo Si counter-attack reaches the city; Wang Yun jumps from the wall to die at the emperor's feet rather than be taken.
Diao Chan; passes to Lu Bu after Dong Zhuo's death - her last appearance as an active force. Her work is done.
Li Ru; arrested at home with Dong Zhuo's other supporters; turned over by his own household and beheaded in the marketplace.

Li Jue; one of Dong Zhuo's four generals who fled to Shanxi after the killing; rallied by Jia Xu's argument that 'die fighting beats die hiding', marches with Guo Si on Changan, breaks the city, and seizes the emperor.
Guo Si; partners Li Jue in the march on Changan; together they break the capital, kill Wang Yun, and take effective control of the emperor.
Fan Chou; among the Dong Zhuo lieutenants who join Li Jue and Guo Si's march.
Zhang Ji; joins Li Jue and Guo Si's march on Changan.

Jia Xu's first appearance - adviser to Li Jue and Guo Si. Tells them flatly that running to Shanxi means death and that uniting Xiliang remnants under a 'revenge for Dong Zhuo' banner gives them a real chance. His plan succeeds and reshapes the next decade.
Li Su; sent in disguise to lure Dong Zhuo into the trap; carries the false abdication edict. Killed shortly afterwards when his role becomes inconvenient.

Cai Yong; weeps openly over Dong Zhuo's body in the marketplace - recalling the patronage Dong Zhuo gave him. Wang Yun has him arrested; he asks only for time to finish his Han history. Strangled in prison.
Niu Fu; Dong Zhuo's son-in-law in command at Meiwo; killed by his own bodyguard Hu Chier when Lu Bu's troops close in. The bodyguard then deserts to Lu Bu with the head.
Hu Chier; Niu Fu's bodyguard who beheads his master to ingratiate himself with Lu Bu - then is killed by Lu Bu in turn for the treachery.
Emperor Xian; passive instrument throughout. Watches Wang Yun jump to his death from the city wall and is taken into 'protection' by Li Jue and Guo Si by chapter's end.
Huangfu Song; among the loyal Han officers caught in the city when Li Jue and Guo Si break in.
Wang Fang; named among the Xiliang officers around Li Jue and Guo Si.
Li Meng; named among the Xiliang officers.
Huang Wan; minister killed alongside Wang Yun when the city falls.
Lu Kui; minister killed in the same purge.
Shisun Rui; minister killed in the same purge.
Dong Huang; Dong Zhuo's nephew; killed in the regime's collapse.
Dong Min; Dong Zhuo's brother; killed in the regime's collapse.
Cui Lie; minister killed in the regime's collapse.
Ding Yuan; named in passing during the recap of Lu Bu's earlier patricidal betrayal.
Ma Midi; minister; recalled to Changan.
Yuan Shu; named in passing in the chapter's catalogue of regional reactions to Dong Zhuo's fall.
Zhou Huan; minor courtier named in passing.
Wang Qin; minor figure named in passing.
Peng Yue; historical reference (Han-founding-era general) cited as a rhetorical example.
Chong Fu; minor reference figure.
King Zhuang of Chu; ancient ruler cited in a moralising aside about Dong Zhuo's tyranny.
Jiang Xiong; rhetorical reference figure.
Liu Che; Emperor Wu of Han; cited in a historical aside on imperial tyranny.
Sima Qian; the Grand Historian; cited in an aside about historical memory.
Zhuge Liang; not yet appeared in the action; named in passing in an authorial aside foreshadowing his future significance.

Ma Teng; arrives with Han Sui and 100,000 Xiliang troops aiming to liberate the emperor from Li Jue and Guo Si. Their inside conspirators (Ma Yu and others) are betrayed; the campaign falls apart, Ma Yu's family is exterminated, and Ma Teng retreats - his son Ma Chao first named here in the field.
Han Sui; partners Ma Teng for the abortive Changan rescue; the failed campaign forces both back to Xiliang.
Ma Chao's debut, age 17; rides at his father's side in the Changan rescue and covers the retreat after the inside conspirators are betrayed.
Ma Yu; Court Counselor among the conspirators with Ma Teng; betrayed and executed with his whole family.
Liu Fan; one of Ma Teng's inside conspirators; killed when the plot is exposed.
Li Jue; receives word of the Ma Teng conspiracy and crushes the rescue army with Guo Si and Jia Xu's help.
Guo Si; partners Li Jue in defeating Ma Teng and Han Sui outside the capital.
Jia Xu; counsels Li Jue and Guo Si against pitched battle, advises starving the Xiliang force out - the strategy that saves the regime.
Li Meng; one of Li Jue's officers in the field.
Wang Fang; one of Li Jue's officers in the field.
Fan Chou; Li Jue's lieutenant; captures Han Sui by guile, lets him go on hometown sentiment, and is murdered for it by Li Jue's nephew Li Bie.
Zhang Ji; among the Xiliang officers around Li Jue and Guo Si.

Pivots fully into Cao Cao's career: takes the Yanzhou governorship after Liu Dai's death, defeats the Qingzhou Yellow Turbans, draws Xun Yu, Xun You, Cheng Yu, Guo Jia, and Yu Jin into his service, and rallies a real army around Yanzhou. Sends for his father Cao Song - the move that triggers the chapter's catastrophe.

Cao Song; Cao Cao's father; en route to Yanzhou is murdered along with his whole household by Zhang Kai's escort, sent ostensibly by Tao Qian. His death is the casus belli for the Xuzhou massacre that follows.
Zhang Kai; the Yellow Turban refugee captain Tao Qian assigns to escort Cao Song's family; slaughters them for the wealth and flees. Vanishes from the novel.

Tao Qian; Imperial Protector of Xuzhou; well-meaning old man whose hospitality goes catastrophically wrong when Zhang Kai murders Cao Song. Sends emissaries trying to explain; Cao Cao's army is already on the march.
Cao Bao; Tao Qian's general defending Xuzhou against Cao Cao's vengeance army - the campaign that will define the early chapters of Liu Bei's rise to Xuzhou rule.
Liu Dai; Imperial Protector of Yanzhou; killed by Yellow Turban remnants - opens the seat that Cao Cao steps into.
Bian Rang; Imperial Protector who recommends Cao Cao as Liu Dai's successor at Yanzhou.
Bao Xin; killed fighting alongside Cao Cao against the Qingzhou Yellow Turbans; Cao Cao mourns him and recruits his troops.
Xun Yu's first appearance - defects from Yuan Shao to Cao Cao with his nephew Xun You; Cao Cao calls him 'my Zifang' (his Zhang Liang). The arrival that sets up Cao Cao's career-long brain trust.
Xun You; arrives at Cao Cao's camp with his uncle Xun Yu and joins the inner council.
Cheng Yu; recommended by Xun Yu and brought into Cao Cao's brain trust in the same wave.
Guo Jia; recommended by Cheng Yu to Cao Cao in the same recruiting wave; becomes Cao Cao's most-trusted strategist until his death in chapter 33.
Liu Ye; brought to Cao Cao by Guo Jia; counsel man in the inner circle.
Man Chong; among the able men Cao Cao recruits in this chapter.
Mao Jie; among the recruits - later admiral of Cao Cao's fleet.
Xiahou Dun; the great recruiter; brings in Yu Jin and others he meets on the road.
Yu Jin; recommended by Xiahou Dun; joins Cao Cao's command and is given the rank that starts his career.
Dian Wei's debut - fearsome strongman of Chenliu who breaks up a tiger's fight to retrieve a runaway flag. Recruited by Xiahou Dun and given to Cao Cao; will become Cao Cao's personal guard captain.
Zhu Jun; mentioned among the imperial officers Cao Cao is now formally above in court rank.
Ying Shao; minor courtier named in passing during Cao Cao's rise.
Yuan Shao; named in passing as the regional power on Cao Cao's northern flank - Xun Yu has just left him.
Dong Zhuo; named in passing as the recently-killed tyrant whose lieutenants Li Jue and Guo Si still hold the emperor.
Zhang Miao; Governor of Chenliu; provisional ally of Cao Cao during the Yanzhou consolidation.
Chen Gong; named in passing in the chapter - his break with Cao Cao after the Lu Boshe killings still lingers.
Liu Xiu; opening-frame reference to the Latter Han founder.
